summary:
The process leads to the development of periodontal pockets due to the characteristic features and pathophysiology associated with apical migration of the epithelial attachment combined with marginal gingival recession as seen in cases of advanced periodontitis or post-treatment conditions where deeper tissue involvement occurs. Key references support these findings indicating it directly corresponds to pocket formation rather than other potential outcomes like root coverage surgery effects which are not aligned here based on provided context clues about disease progression stages involved. To reiterate concisely for clarity:

Apical migration causes deepening gum spaces forming periodontal pockets; no alternative explanation fits observed changes better according to reviewed literature focusing specifically on such clinical scenarios involving connective tissues around teeth undergoing inflammatory processes typical of severe dental diseases progressing through various phases including those after interventions aimed at halting further damage but leaving residual structural alterations visible via probing depths measurements used clinically today.
